claim: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.

evidence.summary: execution_policy declares project_mutation forbidden and restricts allowed_write_roots to evidence dispatch output directory only
evidence.why: This is the core contract that defines the safety boundary: agents can only write to the evidence artifact directory, not to target project files.

evidence.summary: Runtime builds before/after boundary snapshots using buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots, then converts any changes to readiness blockers
evidence.why: This is the enforcement mechanism that detects violations even when agents have full tools. Lines 566-572 show boundary changes become blockers.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function has provider-specific branches for codex and claude that prevent cross-provider leakage
evidence.why: Lines 437-449 clear claude model from codex provider, and lines 450-463 apply claude-specific config only when provider is claude. No cross-contamination occurs.

evidence.summary: Claude provider explicitly clears claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, and claude_disallowed_tools to empty strings
evidence.why: This confirms the design claim that Claude agents receive full tool capability by default, with safety coming from boundary enforcement rather than tool restrictions.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation has separate branches for codex, custom, and claude providers that never mix configuration namespaces
evidence.why: Each provider branch reads only its own configuration keys (codex_model, custom_command, claude_tools) and builds provider-specific args. No cross-provider leakage.

evidence.summary: Test proves codex dispatch does not inherit claude default model: asserts config.ai.provider is codex, config.ai.model is not claude, and codex sandbox settings are independent
evidence.why: Lines 211-215 validate that when provider is codex, the claude model setting is cleared and codex defaults are preserved.

evidence.summary: Test confirms claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags: asserts claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools are all empty strings
evidence.why: Lines 248-253 validate that claude provider runs without tool restrictions, confirming full tool capability is preserved.

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary blocks project file mutation: agent writes a file but boundary detects it and status becomes blocked with BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blocker
evidence.why: Lines 395-410 show that even when agent succeeds and returns valid result, any project mutation triggers a boundary violation blocker.

evidence.summary: Test confirms git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations: creates a git repo, agent writes untracked file, boundary detects the added file
evidence.why: Lines 432-448 validate that the boundary detection works for both tracked and untracked files in git repositories.

evidence.summary: Doctrine states Claude provider dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on prompt contract, artifact root, and boundary snapshots for safety
evidence.why: This is the product-level documentation that explains the design intent: safety comes from boundary enforcement, not from restricting agent tool access.

assumptions: Git ls-files command succeeds in git-initialized project roots during boundary snapshot
Non-git projects use walkFiles which covers the project tree but may miss changes in deeply nested excluded directories
Provider agents respect the prompt contract even when they have full tool capability
risks: If boundary snapshot fails silently, agents could mutate project files without detection
If provider ignores prompt contract and has filesystem write tools, mutations could occur before boundary check runs
Non-git projects may have weaker boundary detection if walkFiles misses changes due to filesystem race conditions or permission errors

result.notes: All explorer evidence claims are independently verified. The implementation has three distinct safety layers: (1) execution_policy contract forbids project mutation and restricts writes to evidence artifact root only, (2) provider-specific configuration branches in executionConfig and buildProviderInvocation prevent cross-provider model/tool leakage, (3) boundary snapshots before/after agent execution detect any violations and convert them to readiness blockers. Tests validate each layer independently. The doctrine documentation confirms this is an intentional design choice: Claude agents receive full tool capability by default, with safety provided by prompt contract and boundary enforcement rather than tool restriction flags.
